title: Introduction to Docker Compose
content: Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications. With Compose, you use a YAML file to configure your application's services. Then, with a single command, you create and start all the services from your configuration.
insight: Compose is the orchestrator for local development.

title: Why use Docker Compose?
content: Managing multiple containers manually using 'docker run' is error-prone and tedious. Compose allows you to document and configure all your application's service dependencies (databases, queues, caches, web engines) in a single file.
insight: Declarative configuration is better than imperative commands.

title: Installing Docker Compose
content: Docker Compose is now integrated into the Docker CLI as 'docker compose' (V2). Previously, it was a standalone Python script called 'docker-compose' (V1). V2 is written in Go and provides better performance and feature parity with the Docker engine.
insight: Use 'docker compose' instead of 'docker-compose' for the latest features.

title: The docker-compose.yml file
content: The heart of Compose is the YAML configuration file. It typically starts with a 'version' (though optional in V2), followed by 'services', 'networks', and 'volumes' sections. Each service represents a container in your application.
insight: YAML's indentation is critical—always use spaces, never tabs.

title: Service Definition Basics
content: A service definition at minimum requires an 'image' or a 'build' instruction. You name the service (e.g., 'web' or 'db'), and this name becomes the hostname for other services in the same network.
insight: Service names are the DNS names within your Compose network.

title: Image vs Build instructions
content: Use 'image' to use an existing image from a registry. Use 'build' to build an image from a Dockerfile in a local directory. You can use both to specify a name for the built image.
insight: Use 'build' during development and 'image' for stable releases.

title: Running 'docker compose up'
content: The 'up' command aggregates the output of each container. When the command exits, all containers are stopped. It builds, (re)creates, starts, and attaches to containers for a service.
insight: 'up' is your primary command for starting everything.

title: Detached Mode (-d)
content: Running 'docker compose up -d' starts the containers in the background and leaves them running. This is the standard way to run services that you don't need to interact with directly in the terminal.
insight: Use detached mode for long-running services like databases.

title: Stopping and Removing with 'down'
content: The 'down' command stops containers and removes containers, networks, images, and volumes defined in the Compose file. By default, only containers and networks are removed.
insight: 'down' cleans up everything created by 'up'.

title: Managing Lifecycle: start and stop
content: Unlike 'up' and 'down', 'start' and 'stop' only affect the container processes without removing them. Use 'stop' to pause work and 'start' to resume exactly where you left off.
insight: 'stop' preserves the container; 'down' destroys it.

title: Viewing Logs
content: Use 'docker compose logs' to see the output from all services. You can use the '-f' flag to follow the logs in real-time, or specify a service name to see logs only for that service.
insight: Logs are essential for debugging multi-container interactions.

title: Executing Commands with 'exec'
content: Similar to 'docker exec', 'docker compose exec' allows you to run commands inside a running service container. It's often used to open a shell (sh or bash) or run database migrations.
insight: 'exec' is for running commands in ALREADY running containers.

title: Environment Variables
content: You can pass environment variables to your services using the 'environment' key in the YAML file. This is crucial for configuring applications without hardcoding values into images.
insight: Keep configuration separate from code using environment variables.

title: The .env file
content: Compose automatically looks for a '.env' file in the project directory. Variables defined here can be used for interpolation in the docker-compose.yml file, keeping secrets out of version control.
insight: Never commit your .env file containing sensitive secrets.

title: Default Networking
content: By default, Compose sets up a single network for your app. Each container for a service joins the default network and is both reachable by other containers on that network, and discoverable by them at a hostname identical to the container name.
insight: Zero-config networking is one of Compose's best features.

title: Custom Networks
content: You can define custom networks in the 'networks' section to isolate services. For example, you might have a 'frontend' and a 'backend' network, with the API service belonging to both but the DB only to 'backend'.
insight: Network isolation improves security and architecture.

title: Connecting Services by Name
content: Because Compose sets up DNS, a web app can connect to a database using 'db:5432' if the database service is named 'db'. No IP addresses are needed.
insight: Always use service names for internal communication.

title: Named Volumes
content: Named volumes are managed by Docker and are the preferred way to persist data. They are defined in the top-level 'volumes' section and then referenced in the service's 'volumes' list.
insight: Volumes are for persistent data, not for code.

title: Bind Mounts in Compose
content: Bind mounts map a host directory to a container directory. In Compose, they are often used in development to sync source code from your host into the container so changes are reflected instantly.
insight: Use bind mounts for development and named volumes for production data.

title: The 'depends_on' instruction
content: 'depends_on' expresses startup and shutdown dependencies between services. It ensures that the 'db' starts before the 'web' app, although it doesn't wait for the 'db' to be 'ready'.
insight: 'depends_on' controls order, but not readiness.

title: Healthchecks in Compose
content: You can define healthchecks in your Compose file to tell Docker how to check if a container is still working. 'depends_on' can also be configured to wait for a service to be 'service_healthy'.
insight: Healthchecks make your startup sequence more robust.

title: Working with Multiple Files
content: Compose supports multiple configuration files. You can use 'docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up' to merge and override configurations for different environments.
insight: Split your config into base and environment-specific files.

title: Extending Services
content: The 'extends' keyword allows you to share common configurations across different services or files. This helps reduce redundancy in large, complex projects.
insight: Keep your YAML DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) with extends.

title: Using Profiles
content: Profiles allow you to group services and only start them when requested. This is useful for optional services like debugging tools or administrative interfaces that aren't needed every time.
insight: Profiles help manage large development environments efficiently.

title: Scaling Services
content: You can scale a service using 'docker compose up --scale web=3'. This starts three instances of the 'web' service. Note that you must avoid host port collisions when scaling.
insight: Scaling is easy as long as you don't hardcode host ports.

title: Resource Limits
content: You can limit the memory and CPU usage of your services using the 'deploy.resources' section. This is important to prevent a single container from starving the host or other containers.
insight: Always set resource limits in multi-tenant environments.

title: Restart Policies
content: The 'restart' key defines whether a container should be restarted automatically if it exits. Options include 'no', 'always', 'on-failure', and 'unless-stopped'.
insight: Use 'unless-stopped' for production services.

title: Using Secrets
content: Compose can manage sensitive data like passwords using 'secrets'. These are mounted as files in '/run/secrets/' inside the container, providing a more secure alternative to environment variables.
insight: Secrets are the secure way to handle credentials.

title: Using Configs
content: Similar to secrets, 'configs' allow you to manage non-sensitive configuration files (like nginx.conf) outside the image. They are mounted as files in the container.
insight: Decouple configuration files from your application images.

title: Compose V2 'develop' and 'watch'
content: New in Compose V2, the 'develop' section allows for 'watch' mode. It automatically syncs files or rebuilds containers when local source code changes, providing a seamless dev experience.
insight: 'watch' mode replaces the need for external file-watching tools.

title: Development vs Production
content: Your development Compose file should focus on speed (bind mounts, hot-reloading). Your production file should focus on security and reliability (read-only filesystems, resource limits, secrets).
insight: Use overrides to tailor Compose to your environment.

title: Variable Substitution
content: Compose supports shell-style variable substitution. You can use '${VARIABLE}' in your YAML file, and Compose will replace it with the value from your environment or .env file.
insight: Dynamic YAML files make your setup more flexible.

title: YAML Anchors and Aliases
content: You can use YAML anchors (&) and aliases (*) to reuse snippets of configuration within the same file. This is great for sharing common environment variables across many services.
insight: Anchors reduce duplication within a single YAML file.

title: The 'init' flag
content: Setting 'init: true' in a service definition tells Docker to use an init process (like tini) as PID 1. This ensures zombie processes are reaped and signals are handled correctly.
insight: Use 'init' for applications that don't handle signals well.

title: Controlling Startup Order
content: While 'depends_on' helps, it often isn't enough. Using scripts like 'wait-for-it.sh' inside your container entrypoint ensures the app only starts when the DB is actually accepting connections.
insight: Apps should be resilient to their dependencies being unavailable.

title: Labels in Compose
content: Labels are metadata you can attach to containers, networks, and volumes. Tools like Traefik use these labels for dynamic routing and service discovery.
insight: Labels are the key to automated infrastructure.

title: Pulling and Pushing
content: You can use 'docker compose pull' to fetch the latest images for your services, and 'docker compose push' if you have built images that need to be uploaded to a registry.
insight: Compose can manage your entire image lifecycle.

title: Service Status (ps and top)
content: 'docker compose ps' lists the status of containers in your project. 'docker compose top' shows the running processes within those containers.
insight: Use 'ps' to quickly check if your stack is healthy.

title: Best Practices
content: Keep services small, use .dockerignore to keep builds fast, name your volumes, and use profiles for optional components. Always document your setup in a README.
insight: A clean Compose file is a happy Compose file.

title: Summary of Docker Compose
content: Docker Compose bridges the gap between single-container runs and full orchestration. It's the standard tool for local development and simple production deployments.
insight: Mastering Compose is essential for any modern developer.
